content.text: Knowing Where Your Data Came From — and Where It's Going

In any data pipeline, a bug that corrupts a column or drops rows can silently poison every report and model that depends on it. Without a map of how data flows — what produced it, what transformed it, what consumes it — debugging becomes archaeology.
is the library that stores it so every stakeholder can find and trust the data they need.
Together, lineage and cataloging form the backbone of
: the ability to understand the health, origin, and meaning of data at every stage of a pipeline, not just whether the pipeline ran.
is the full record of a piece of data's lifecycle: where it originated, every transformation it passed through, and every downstream system that consumed it. It answers the questions
"If I change this table, what breaks?"
is the organized, searchable index of all data assets in an organization — tables, columns, schemas, pipelines, owners, quality metrics, and tags — along with the lineage that connects them.
They are complementary: lineage without a catalog is a graph with no labels; a catalog without lineage is a library with no index of how books relate to each other.

Modern data platforms are not single pipelines. They are webs of ingestion jobs, transformation layers (dbt, Spark, pandas, SQL), feature stores, ML training scripts, dashboards, and APIs — all reading from and writing to overlapping datasets. Without lineage and cataloging:
A broken upstream table silently corrupts five dashboards before anyone notices.
Data engineers spend days tracing hand-written notes and Slack messages to understand a schema.
Compliance teams cannot answer "show me every system that touched customer PII."
ML engineers train models on stale or misunderstood feature definitions.
Schema changes break downstream jobs with no warning.
Lineage and cataloging convert this chaos into a queryable, auditable, observable system.
table B was derived from table A
. This is the coarse-grained view — useful for impact analysis but not enough for debugging.
. This is the fine-grained view needed for root cause analysis when a single metric is wrong.

Most production systems need both. Column-level lineage is significantly harder to capture automatically because it requires parsing SQL or transformation code semantically, not just tracking table names.
Which tables derive from which tables
Which columns derive from which columns
Which source rows produced which output rows
Row 42 in output ← rows 7, 19 in input
Row-level lineage is extremely expensive to store and is rarely done outside of compliance-specific systems.
There are three primary strategies for capturing lineage:

parses SQL, dbt YAML, or Python ASTs before runtime to extract dependencies. It is fast and requires no instrumentation, but can miss dynamic queries, runtime branching, or code that constructs SQL strings.

intercepts actual I/O calls — database connections, file reads/writes, API calls — as the pipeline executes. It captures the real execution graph but adds overhead and requires wrapping or patching libraries.

has the pipeline itself emit lineage events to a collector (e.g., calling an OpenLineage API after each step). This is explicit and reliable but requires developer discipline to implement consistently.
Production systems typically combine all three.
How Lineage Works in a Python Pipeline
is the de facto open standard for lineage events. It defines a JSON schema for a
(the pipeline step, identified by namespace + name)
(datasets written, with schema snapshots and row-count stats)
This standard means lineage events from Spark, Airflow, dbt, and a custom Python script can all be sent to the same collector (like Marquez or DataHub) and assembled into a unified graph.
Below is a complete example of a Python pipeline step that manually emits OpenLineage-compatible events using the

This pattern — START → transform → COMPLETE (or FAIL) — is the core loop of any lineage-instrumented job. Every run produces a UUID that links the run to the lineage event, enabling time-travel debugging: you can query "show me the lineage graph for
of this job on Tuesday at 3 AM."
package automatically intercepts DAG runs and emits lineage events for many operator types (SQL operators, S3 operators, BigQuery operators) without any manual instrumentation:

# airflow/dags/orders_pipeline.py
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.providers.postgres.operators.postgres import PostgresOperator
from datetime import datetime

# With the OpenLineage provider installed and OPENLINEAGE_URL set in env,
# this DAG automatically emits lineage for the SQL transformation below.
with DAG("orders_pipeline", start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1), schedule="@daily") as dag:

    enrich_orders = PostgresOperator(
        task_id="enrich_orders_with_currency",
        postgres_conn_id="warehouse",
        sql="""
            INSERT INTO gold.fact_orders (order_id, revenue_usd)
            SELECT
                o.order_id,
                o.amount_raw / fx.rate_to_usd AS revenue_usd
            FROM raw.orders o
            JOIN raw.exchange_rates fx USING (currency_code);
        """,
    )
The provider parses the SQL statically and emits an OpenLineage event with
as the output, including the column-level derivation of
at compile time containing the full DAG of models, their SQL, source tables, and column-level dependencies. Tools like
ingest this manifest to build lineage automatically:

# dbt compiles manifest.json which encodes full column-level lineage
dbt compile --profiles-dir . --project-dir .

# DataHub CLI ingests the manifest
datahub ingest -c dbt_ingestion.yml

# dbt_ingestion.yml
source:
  type: dbt
  config:
    manifest_path: ./target/manifest.json
    catalog_path: ./target/catalog.json
    target_platform: postgres

sink:
  type: datahub-rest
  config:
    server: "http://localhost:8080"
This single ingestion step pulls every model, its columns, its upstream sources, and its SQL logic into the catalog with zero additional annotation in Python code.

stores the metadata about every dataset that lineage produces: schema definitions, ownership, quality scores, tags, business glossary terms, and the lineage graph itself. Cataloging serves two audiences: engineers who need technical metadata and business users who need to discover and understand data.

Dataset: gold.fact_orders
───────────────────────────────────────────────
Owner:          data-engineering@company.com
Domain:         Finance / Revenue
Updated:        2024-06-10 03:14 UTC (by job: transform.orders.enrich_with_currency)
Row count:      4,821,339
Last run:       SUCCESS (run_id: a7f3c...)

Columns:
  order_id     BIGINT   PK  — Unique order identifier
  revenue_usd  NUMERIC      — Order revenue normalized to USD

Tags: [PII:None] [SLA:Gold] [Team:Analytics]

Upstream:
  raw.orders          ← ingestion.postgres.orders
  raw.exchange_rates  ← ingestion.fixer_api

Downstream:
  reporting.revenue_by_region   (dashboard: Revenue Overview)
  ml.training.ltv_model_features
  api.order_summary_endpoint

Quality:
  revenue_usd  null_rate=0.00%  min=0.01  max=184,230.50
This entry tells a data consumer everything they need to know: where the data came from, who owns it, what each column means, who consumes it, and whether it is healthy.
Registering Datasets in Python with DataHub
DataHub provides a Python emitter that sends metadata directly to its REST API. This can be called from pipeline code to keep the catalog synchronized with actual runs:

from datahub.emitter.rest_emitter import DatahubRestEmitter
from datahub.emitter.mcp import MetadataChangeProposalWrapper
from datahub.metadata.schema_classes import (
    DatasetPropertiesClass,
    SchemaMetadataClass,
    SchemaFieldClass,
    SchemaFieldDataTypeClass,
    NumberTypeClass,
    StringTypeClass,
    OtherSchemaClass,
    DatasetLineageTypeClass,
    UpstreamLineageClass,
    UpstreamClass,
)
from datahub.metadata.com.linkedin.pegasus2avro.common import AuditStamp

emitter = DatahubRestEmitter(gms_server="http://localhost:8080")

PLATFORM = "urn:li:dataPlatform:postgres"

def register_dataset(dataset_name: str, description: str, columns: list[dict]):
    """
    Registers a dataset in DataHub with schema and description.
    columns: [{"name": "order_id", "type": "long", "description": "..."}, ...]
    """
    dataset_urn = f"urn:li:dataset:({PLATFORM},{dataset_name},PROD)"

    # Emit dataset properties
    properties_event = MetadataChangeProposalWrapper(
        entityUrn=dataset_urn,
        aspect=DatasetPropertiesClass(description=description),
    )
    emitter.emit(properties_event)

    # Emit schema
    fields = []
    type_map = {
        "long": SchemaFieldDataTypeClass(type=NumberTypeClass()),
        "double": SchemaFieldDataTypeClass(type=NumberTypeClass()),
        "string": SchemaFieldDataTypeClass(type=StringTypeClass()),
    }
    for col in columns:
        fields.append(SchemaFieldClass(
            fieldPath=col["name"],
            type=type_map.get(col["type"], SchemaFieldDataTypeClass(type=StringTypeClass())),
            nativeDataType=col["type"],
            description=col.get("description", ""),
        ))

    schema_event = MetadataChangeProposalWrapper(
        entityUrn=dataset_urn,
        aspect=SchemaMetadataClass(
            schemaName=dataset_name,
            platform=PLATFORM,
            version=0,
            hash="",
            platformSchema=OtherSchemaClass(rawSchema=""),
            fields=fields,
        ),
    )
    emitter.emit(schema_event)
    print(f"Registered {dataset_name} in DataHub catalog")


def register_lineage(output_dataset: str, input_datasets: list[str]):
    """Registers upstream lineage for a dataset."""
    output_urn = f"urn:li:dataset:({PLATFORM},{output_dataset},PROD)"
    upstreams = [
        UpstreamClass(
            dataset=f"urn:li:dataset:({PLATFORM},{ds},PROD)",
            type=DatasetLineageTypeClass.TRANSFORMED,
        )
        for ds in input_datasets
    ]
    lineage_event = MetadataChangeProposalWrapper(
        entityUrn=output_urn,
        aspect=UpstreamLineageClass(upstreams=upstreams),
    )
    emitter.emit(lineage_event)


# Usage after pipeline step completes:
register_dataset(
    dataset_name="gold.fact_orders",
    description="Order revenue normalized to USD. Joins raw orders with daily FX rates.",
    columns=[
        {"name": "order_id", "type": "long", "description": "Unique order identifier"},
        {"name": "revenue_usd", "type": "double", "description": "Order amount in USD"},
    ]
)
register_lineage(
    output_dataset="gold.fact_orders",
    input_datasets=["raw.orders", "raw.exchange_rates"]
)
Using a Lightweight In-Process Catalog with

For smaller pipelines that do not yet use a dedicated catalog tool, a schema registry can be encoded directly in Python using Pydantic models. This does not replace a real catalog but provides schema validation and self-documentation:
This pattern enforces a contract at every pipeline stage, making data quality violations loud and explicit rather than silent.
Architecture: End-to-End Lineage and Catalog System

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        DATA SOURCES                                  │
│   PostgreSQL   ·   S3   ·   Kafka   ·   External APIs               │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ raw data
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     INGESTION LAYER                                  │
│   Python ingest jobs / Airbyte / Fivetran                            │
│   → emit OpenLineage START/COMPLETE events per run                   │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ bronze tables + lineage events
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  TRANSFORMATION LAYER                                │
│   dbt models / Spark / pandas Python jobs                            │
│   → static lineage extracted from dbt manifest.json                 │
│   → runtime lineage emitted via openlineage-python / Airflow         │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ silver/gold tables + column-level lineage
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               LINEAGE & CATALOG BACKEND                              │
│   OpenLineage Collector (Marquez) — stores run events                │
│   Data Catalog (DataHub / OpenMetadata) — stores metadata + graph   │
│   Ingestion connectors pull from: dbt, Airflow, Spark, S3, DBs      │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ queryable lineage graph + catalog API
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CONSUMPTION LAYER                                 │
│   Engineers: impact analysis, root cause debugging                   │
│   Data Scientists: dataset discovery, feature lineage                │
│   Compliance: PII audit trails, GDPR data mapping                   │
│   Dashboards/APIs: BI tools, ML models, product APIs                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
The lineage graph flows upward through layers; the catalog aggregates metadata from every layer and exposes a unified search and query interface.
Implementing a Lineage Decorator in Python
A practical pattern for Python pipelines is a
that automatically wraps any transformation function, emits START/COMPLETE/FAIL events, captures row counts, and registers the result in the catalog — all without modifying the function's core logic:

import functools
import uuid
import time
import logging
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Callable, Any

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def track_lineage(
    job_name: str,
    inputs: list[str],
    outputs: list[str],
    namespace: str = "data-platform.production"
):
    """
    Decorator that wraps a transformation function with OpenLineage event emission.

    Usage:
        @track_lineage(
            job_name="transform.orders.enrich",
            inputs=["raw.orders", "raw.exchange_rates"],
            outputs=["gold.fact_orders"],
        )
        def enrich_orders(raw_df, exchange_df):
            ...
            return result_df
    """
    def decorator(fn: Callable) -> Callable:
        @functools.wraps(fn)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs) -> Any:
            run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
            start_time = time.monotonic()

            logger.info(
                "Lineage START",
                extra={
                    "run_id": run_id,
                    "job": job_name,
                    "inputs": inputs,
                    "outputs": outputs,
                }
            )

            _emit_openlineage_event(
                namespace=namespace,
                job_name=job_name,
                run_id=run_id,
                inputs=inputs,
                outputs=[],
                state="START",
            )

            try:
                result = fn(*args, **kwargs)
                elapsed = time.monotonic() - start_time

                # Capture row count if result is a DataFrame
                row_count = None
                try:
                    row_count = len(result)
                except TypeError:
                    pass

                logger.info(
                    "Lineage COMPLETE",
                    extra={
                        "run_id": run_id,
                        "job": job_name,
                        "elapsed_seconds": round(elapsed, 3),
                        "output_rows": row_count,
                    }
                )

                _emit_openlineage_event(
                    namespace=namespace,
                    job_name=job_name,
                    run_id=run_id,
                    inputs=inputs,
                    outputs=outputs,
                    state="COMPLETE",
                    row_count=row_count,
                )
                return result

            except Exception as exc:
                elapsed = time.monotonic() - start_time
                logger.error(
                    "Lineage FAIL",
                    extra={
                        "run_id": run_id,
                        "job": job_name,
                        "error": str(exc),
                        "elapsed_seconds": round(elapsed, 3),
                    },
                    exc_info=True,
                )
                _emit_openlineage_event(
                    namespace=namespace,
                    job_name=job_name,
                    run_id=run_id,
                    inputs=inputs,
                    outputs=[],
                    state="FAIL",
                )
                raise

        return wrapper
    return decorator


def _emit_openlineage_event(
    namespace: str,
    job_name: str,
    run_id: str,
    inputs: list[str],
    outputs: list[str],
    state: str,
    row_count: int | None = None,
):
    """Sends an OpenLineage event. Replace body with actual client call."""
    event_payload = {
        "eventType": state,
        "eventTime": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "run": {"runId": run_id},
        "job": {"namespace": namespace, "name": job_name},
        "inputs": [{"namespace": namespace, "name": ds} for ds in inputs],
        "outputs": [
            {
                "namespace": namespace,
                "name": ds,
                **({"facets": {"rowCount": row_count}} if row_count else {}),
            }
            for ds in outputs
        ],
    }
    # In production: client.emit(RunEvent(**event_payload))
    logger.debug("OpenLineage event: %s", event_payload)


# --- Using the decorator ---

@track_lineage(
    job_name="transform.orders.enrich_with_currency",
    inputs=["raw.orders", "raw.exchange_rates"],
    outputs=["gold.fact_orders"],
)
def enrich_orders(raw_df, exchange_df):
    merged = raw_df.merge(exchange_df, on="currency_code")
    merged["revenue_usd"] = merged["amount_raw"] / merged["rate_to_usd"]
    return merged[["order_id", "revenue_usd"]]
This decorator keeps lineage logic completely separate from business logic, making it easy to add to any existing transformation function.
A mid-scale e-commerce platform processes 5 million orders per day through this pipeline:

PostgreSQL (OLTP)
   orders table, payments table, products table
         ↓  (Python ingest job, runs hourly)
S3 Bronze Layer
   raw_orders.parquet, raw_payments.parquet
         ↓  (dbt + Spark, runs hourly)
Snowflake Silver Layer
   clean_orders (deduped, validated)
   clean_payments (reconciled)
         ↓  (dbt, runs hourly)
Snowflake Gold Layer
   fact_orders (revenue_usd, joined with FX rates)
   dim_customer (aggregated customer lifetime value)
         ↓
Looker dashboards + ML training jobs + product API
dashboard drops 12% with no obvious cause. The on-call engineer opens DataHub, clicks on
, and views its lineage graph. In 30 seconds they see:
, which was last updated 48 hours ago — the FX API ingestion job silently failed with a 429 rate-limit error.
Because the FX table was stale, the USD conversion used an exchange rate from 48 hours ago, artificially deflating revenue for orders in EUR and GBP during the USD strength window.
, this investigation would have taken hours of SQL archaeology. With lineage, the COMPLETE audit trail from the FX API failure to the dashboard metric is visible in the catalog in under a minute.
The FX ingestion job is patched to retry on 429s, alert on stale data, and emit a lineage FAIL event visible in the catalog when it does not refresh within its SLA window.
If jobs only emit COMPLETE events, the lineage graph has no record of failures. Always emit START and FAIL events too, so the graph shows broken nodes visually.
is insufficient for debugging a single column. Column-level lineage is critical for understanding metric discrepancies.
Lineage without run IDs makes it impossible to distinguish between yesterday's run and today's run of the same job. Every execution must have a UUID.
Treating the catalog as a one-time project.

Catalogs become stale almost immediately if there is no automated ingestion pipeline keeping them synchronized with actual schemas and job runs. A stale catalog is worse than no catalog — it creates false confidence.
Datasets without clear owners become orphans that nobody maintains. Every dataset entry must have a named owner (team or individual) with a contact path.
If the dataset name in the lineage event (
) does not exactly match the name in the catalog (
), the graph breaks into disconnected fragments. Establish and enforce a naming convention across all tools.
When a column is renamed or dropped, the lineage graph must be updated. Without schema change tracking, the graph silently becomes incorrect as columns shift over time.
Adopt OpenLineage as the standard from day one.

Using a vendor-specific lineage format locks lineage into a single tool. OpenLineage events work with Marquez, DataHub, OpenMetadata, Atlan, and any other OpenLineage-compatible backend interchangeably.
Automate catalog ingestion, never do it manually.

Connect dbt, Airflow, and Spark directly to the catalog via connectors. Manually maintained catalog entries drift immediately. Treat catalog entries the same as infrastructure-as-code: they must be generated, not written by hand.
Emit lineage as close to I/O as possible.

The lineage event should fire at the same point the data is written — ideally in the same transaction or immediately after. Emitting lineage in a separate cleanup job introduces lag and the possibility of missing events if the job fails.
Tag PII columns in the catalog immediately.
PII column tags should be emitted as part of schema registration, not added retroactively. Use tags like
so compliance queries can find all PII-bearing columns across the entire graph instantly.
Set SLAs on datasets and monitor them.

Every gold-layer dataset should have a freshness SLA (e.g., "must be updated within 2 hours of midnight UTC"). The catalog should alert when the SLA is breached, not wait for a dashboard user to notice stale data.
Use the catalog for impact analysis before schema changes.
Before dropping or renaming a column, query the catalog's downstream lineage to enumerate every consumer. Contact owners of downstream jobs before making the change.
Store data quality metrics alongside lineage.
Row counts, null rates, and value distributions for each run should be stored as lineage facets. This enables anomaly detection: if today's
has 40% fewer rows than yesterday's, that should trigger an alert before any downstream consumer sees the data.
Lightweight, REST API, ideal for OpenLineage testing and small setups
Full catalog + lineage, rich UI, large ecosystem of connectors
Strong data quality integration, active community
Enterprise-grade, deep dbt/Airflow integration, business glossary
Emit OpenLineage events from Python code
Python emitter and ingestion framework for DataHub
Data quality checks; integrates with catalog tools for quality metadata
Schema validation and self-documenting data contracts in Python
Parses SQL strings to extract table and column-level lineage

from sqllineage.runner import LineageRunner

sql = """
INSERT INTO gold.fact_orders (order_id, revenue_usd)
SELECT
    o.order_id,
    o.amount_raw / fx.rate_to_usd AS revenue_usd
FROM raw.orders o
JOIN raw.exchange_rates fx ON o.currency_code = fx.currency_code
"""

runner = LineageRunner(sql)

print("Source tables:", runner.source_tables())
# Source tables: [<Table: raw.exchange_rates>, <Table: raw.orders>]

print("Target tables:", runner.target_tables())
# Target tables: [<Table: gold.fact_orders>]

print("Column lineage:")
for lineage in runner.get_column_lineage():
    print(f"  {lineage}")
# <Column: raw.orders.order_id> -> <Column: gold.fact_orders.order_id>
# <Column: raw.orders.amount_raw> -> <Column: gold.fact_orders.revenue_usd>
# <Column: raw.exchange_rates.rate_to_usd> -> <Column: gold.fact_orders.revenue_usd>
source columns — a relationship invisible at the table level — extracted purely from SQL without running the query.
The most robust production setup combines
OpenLineage for runtime lineage from Python/Spark/Airflow jobs
as the unified catalog that ingests both.

The lineage graph reveals which tables contain PII, how compliance-sensitive data flows, and the full architecture of the data platform. Access to the catalog must be role-controlled — engineers should see technical metadata, compliance officers should see PII tags and audit trails, and business users should see business glossary and ownership, but not necessarily raw schemas.
Lineage systems must not block pipelines.

The lineage emitter client should use a fire-and-forget pattern or an async queue. If the OpenLineage collector goes down, the pipeline itself should not fail. Use a circuit breaker or a local queue (e.g., writing events to a local file or a Kafka topic) that can replay when the collector recovers:

When a source table's schema changes (a column is dropped or its type changes), the lineage graph should detect the mismatch and raise an alert before downstream jobs fail. DataHub and OpenMetadata both support schema change detection via periodic schema fingerprint comparison.

Run-level lineage events can accumulate at high volume (one event per job execution, per run). Define a retention policy: keep full lineage for 90 days, roll up to aggregated lineage summaries for longer retention. Most catalog tools support configurable retention.

The catalog is a critical operational tool, not just documentation. Design it for high availability — run it behind a load balancer, use a replicated database backend, and treat its SLA as seriously as the pipelines it describes.
Data lineage is the map of how data moves and transforms; data cataloging is the searchable library that makes that map queryable and discoverable.
Column-level lineage is essential for debugging individual metric errors; table-level lineage alone is insufficient for production systems.
OpenLineage is the open standard for emitting lineage events across Python, Airflow, Spark, and dbt — adopt it early to avoid vendor lock-in.
Every pipeline transformation should emit START, COMPLETE, and FAIL lineage events with a unique run ID to enable time-travel debugging.
Catalogs go stale instantly without automated ingestion — treat catalog synchronization as a first-class pipeline job, not a manual task.
Quality metrics (row counts, null rates, value ranges) should be stored as lineage facets alongside each run, enabling anomaly detection before consumers are impacted.
Lineage emission must never block or fail a pipeline — use fire-and-forget patterns with circuit breakers so collector outages do not propagate to production jobs.
